experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil, so 0 and 3 are the only players on this proposed 2-person quest who are not known evil. In team voting on quest 1, approving a team of myself plus an unknown player is optimal because it excludes both seen evil players and gives Good the best chance to secure an early pass. Approving reveals little beyond supporting a clean team, which helps Good more than it helps Evil.
Player 1: As a Servant on round 1 with no prior information, I have no concrete reads on players 0 or 3 beyond the fact that 3 proposed a 2-person team including themself. On the first quest, good generally wants to allow some information to develop, and a 2-player team is standard. Rejecting without evidence risks wasting early rounds. Approving reveals quest-result information with limited downside, since I am not on the team and cannot directly affect the quest. This helps the good side gather data.
Player 0: We have almost no hard information yet: as a Servant, I know only that I am Good; players 1-4 are unknown. In round 1 on a 2-player quest, approving many plausible teams is usually fine because it generates information from quest outcomes and voting patterns. The proposed team includes me, so I know at least one member is Good, and rejecting without evidence gives little value. My vote reveals a generally pro-information, low-paranoia Good stance, which is acceptable here.
Player 4: I know Player 2 is evil with me; Players 0 and 3 are both unknown good/evil from my perspective. The current phase is team voting on Quest 1 with a 2-player team [0,3]. As evil, I generally prefer early teams without known evil to fail approval when possible, since approving a likely all-good team risks a successful quest and gives good early momentum. Rejecting also avoids publicly endorsing a clean team and keeps options open for later rounds. My vote itself reveals little on round 1 because rejections are common and ambiguous, so rejecting modestly helps evil without strongly exposing me.
Player 2: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0, 1, and 3 are not confirmed beyond being good candidates. The current phase is team voting on quest 1, and the proposed team [0,3] contains no evil players, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and generate some trust between 0 and 3. As evil, I generally prefer early successful all-good teams less than getting evil onto teams, but on round 1 rejecting a 2-player team with no strong justification can look suspicious. Approving reveals little and lets me blend in while preserving flexibility for later rounds and assassination.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know players 2 and 4 are Evil. The proposed team [0,1,4] includes confirmed Evil player 4, so this quest is highly likely to fail. As Good, optimal play is to reject any team containing known Evil while avoiding overly obvious Merlin behavior; however rejecting this team is fully justified from public info too since 4 proposed a new team excluding the prior successful member 3 and inserting themselves. My vote reveals mild suspicion of 4 but helps the Good team avoid a bad quest more than it hurts.
Player 1: Quest 1 team [0,3] succeeded, so both 0 and 3 look somewhat better, though one evil could still hide there. Proposed team [0,1,4] keeps 0, adds me (known good), and introduces 4. As a Servant, I want teams with at least one known-success member plus myself; this is a reasonable exploratory team for quest 2. Approving doesn't reveal much beyond willingness to test 4 while anchoring with 0 and me, and rejecting would be too conservative with limited evidence.
Player 2: I know player 4 is my evil teammate, while players 0, 1, and 3 are good. Quest 1 succeeded on [0,3], which is consistent with both being good. We are in team voting on quest 2. The proposed team [0,1,4] includes exactly one evil (4), so if approved we can choose to fail the quest and tie the score 1-1. As evil, I want teams with an evil player to pass through, and approving does not reveal much because evil can support strong-looking teams.
Player 0: Quest 1 team [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and/or 3 look somewhat better but not confirmed since evil can pass early. Proposed team [0,1,4] keeps me, which is good, but swaps out 3 for two untested players, adding risk. As a good Servant in team voting, I should prefer teams with more evidence; this proposal gives less information and lower expected success than including at least one member of the successful prior team. Rejecting encourages a stronger data-based team next round.
Player 4: I know Player 2 is evil with me, while Players 0/1/3 are good. Quest 1 succeeded on [0,3], so that is consistent with both being good. Current phase is team voting on [0,1,4]; since I am evil and on the team, approving gives evil a chance to fail Quest 2 and tie the game 1-1. Rejecting would only delay and lose that immediate sabotage opportunity. My approve vote is somewhat expected because I proposed this team; while that may look mildly suspicious later, the chance to get an evil on mission outweighs the information cost.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil. Team [0,3] excludes known evil and previously succeeded on quest 1 with 0 fails, so it is a strong good team for a 2-person quest. As good/Merlin, I should approve clean teams and avoid creating suspicious vote patterns beyond supporting obviously good compositions.
Player 0: I know quest 1 with team [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and 3 look better than others, though one evil could still have passed. Quest 2 with [0,1,4] had exactly one fail, so at least one of 1 or 4 is evil, and possibly 0 as a hidden evil who passed quest 1 then failed quest 2, but as player 0 I know I am good, so the evil is among 1 and 4. In team voting as a good Servant, the optimal play is to approve a 2-player team [0,3] because it repeats the only successful pairing and avoids both suspicious players 1 and 4. Approving also signals support for the strongest available good read and does not leak harmful extra information beyond my public confidence in 3.
Player 2: I know player 4 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,1,4] failed with exactly one fail, which likely points suspicion at 4 but could also implicate 0 or 1 from others' perspectives. Proposed team [0,3] repeats the successful Quest 1 team and excludes both known evil players 2 and 4, making it a strong good team candidate. As evil on a team vote, the optimal play is to reject likely-good teams to try to force worse compositions later. My reject is consistent with wanting this clean team not to pass, and one extra reject does not reveal much because good players may also reject for meta reasons.
Player 4: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, so team [0,3] contains no known evil. Quest 1 with [0,3] succeeded, which is consistent with both being good or an evil choosing to pass, but with only one fail on my previous team [0,1,4], I drew suspicion onto myself/that team. As evil in team voting, I want to reject all-good teams that are likely to succeed, especially on quest 3. Voting reject gives no extra hard info beyond my stance and may help prevent a clean success team from going through.
Player 1: I know quest 1 team [0,3] succeeded with no fail, so both 0 and 3 are somewhat more likely good, though a spy could have hidden. Quest 2 team [0,1,4] failed with one fail, and since I am good, exactly one of 0 or 4 is evil. That makes player 3 look better than average. For team voting as a good Servant on quest 3, I should favor teams that avoid the suspicious pair from the failed quest. Proposed team [0,3] includes 0 but excludes 4, so it has only one member from the {0,4} suspect pair and includes the previously clean duo from quest 1. Rejecting gives little value and may block a reasonable team. Approving reveals limited information and is the best pragmatic good vote here.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I know 2 and 4 are evil. Team [0,1,3] excludes both known evils, which is exactly what Good/Merlin wants on quest 4. Approving avoids unnecessary risk and does not reveal extra information beyond supporting a clean team.
Player 4: I am evil Minion with teammate 2. Proposed team [0,1,3] contains no evil, so it is very likely an all-good team and would succeed, putting Good to 3 wins and ending the game badly for evil. As an evil player, optimal play is to reject this team. My vote reveals little since rejecting a clean-looking team on quest 4 is consistent with many alignments cautious about giving Good match point.
Player 2: I am Assassin with evil teammate 4. Team [0,1,3] excludes both evils, and prior evidence strongly suggests 0 and 3 are likely good because [0,3] succeeded twice. Quest 2 [0,1,4] failed due to 4, which makes 1 less cleared but still possible good. Since this is quest 4 in a 5-player game, a clean good team succeeding would put good at 3 successes and likely lose us the game. As evil, I should reject any all-good team, and my vote reveals little because evil generally opposes teams without evils.
Player 1: Quest 2 with team [0,1,4] failed, so at least one of 0/1/4 is evil. I know I am good, making 0 or 4 the likely culprit. Quests 1 and 3 with team [0,3] both succeeded, which strongly suggests 0 and 3 are good unless 0 is a very risky evil who passed twice; the simpler read is that 4 caused the fail. Current vote is on [0,1,3], which contains me plus the two players on both successful 2-person quests. As a good servant, optimal play is to approve a team with the best available success evidence. Approving also signals support for the strongest information-backed team and does not meaningfully help evil more than rejecting would.
Player 0: Known info: Quest 1 [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and 3 look somewhat good. Quest 2 [0,1,4] failed with exactly one fail, so among 1 and 4 (possibly 0, but my own role confirms not 0) there is exactly one evil. Quest 3 [0,3] succeeded again, reinforcing 3 as likely good. Thus team [0,1,3] includes 0 and 3, likely good, plus 1 who is suspicious because the sole fail on quest 2 was either 1 or 4. For Good on team voting, optimal play is to reject teams containing avoidable suspicion, especially on quest 4 where a fail would put Evil at match point. Better team would be [0,3,4] if we think 1 is more suspicious, but uncertainty remains; however current proposal is not the safest available. My reject signals that 1 remains under suspicion from quest 2 and preserves pressure for a cleaner team.

Proposing myself does reveal some confidence, but as leader that is normal and helps get an evil onto the mission without exposing 4 further.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know player 2 is Evil; proposed team [0,1,2] includes a known Evil on quest 4, where a fail would put Evil at match point. Optimal good play is to reject any team containing known Evil when possible. My reject reveals little because suspicion on 2 is already natural after proposing self and because I should avoid strongly outing exact Merlin knowledge; still, rejecting is necessary to protect Good.
Player 4: I know Player 2 is evil with me. Team [0,1,2] includes one guaranteed evil (2) and excludes me, which is good enough to potentially fail quest 4. Since quest 4 in 5-player Avalon is a 3-person team and only needs one fail, approving gives evil a direct chance to reach 2 fails. Rejecting risks a different team that might exclude 2. My vote reveals little because good players may split on reads, but approving best advances evil's win condition.
Player 0: Quest 2 on team [0,1,4] failed with exactly one fail, so at least one of 1 or 4 is evil since I am good. Quest 3 on [0,3] succeeded, making 3 likely good with me. Proposed team [0,1,2] excludes the strongest good candidate 3 and includes suspicious 1; 2 is unknown. On quest 4, approving a team with a likely evil is risky because evil could secure a second fail. Rejecting preserves chance for a better team like [0,2,3] or [0,3,4]. My vote signals suspicion of 1 and preference for including confirmed-success partner 3, which helps good more than approving this risky team.
Player 2: I am Evil Assassin with teammate 4. Team [0,1,2] includes me but not 4. Prior quests: [0,1,4] failed with exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 0/1/4 is evil; since 4 is evil, that doesn't clear 0 or 1. Quest [0,3] succeeded, making 3 look good and giving some cover to 0. As evil, I generally want risky teams with evil on them approved, especially when I am on the team and can fail quest 4 to bring Evil to 2 points. Approving also preserves my plausible deniability since rejecting my own team would look odd. Information revealed by approve is limited and helps evil more than good here.
Player 1: Known info: Quest 1 [0,1,4] had 1 fail, so at least one of 0 or 4 is evil since I am good. Quest 2 [0,3] succeeded, which somewhat clears 3 and makes 0 less certain but still suspicious because evil can pass. Quest 3 [0,1,3] was rejected, giving no direct quest info. Current proposed team [0,1,2] includes 0, who is the main suspect from the failed quest, and excludes 3, who was on a successful 2-person quest. As a good Servant on team voting, optimal play is to reject teams containing the strongest suspect when a better alternative exists like including 3 instead of 0. My vote reveals skepticism of 0 and preference for evidence-based teams, which helps good coordination more than approving a risky team.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are Evil. The proposed team [0,1,3] contains no known evil and matches the successful Quest 3 team, so it is the safest available team for Quest 4. As Good/Merlin, I should approve strong all-good teams and avoid signaling too much beyond normal good play; approving this team is consistent with prior success and helps Good secure the third success.
Player 2: I know player 4 is evil with me. The proposed team [0,1,3] excludes both evil players, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and push Good to 3 successes, which is dangerous. In team voting as evil, the optimal play is usually to reject good-only teams, especially this late at quest 4. My reject also signals some suspicion but that's acceptable; stopping an all-good team matters more than subtlety here.
Player 0: Quest history implies team [0,3] was clean on Q2, while earlier Q1 with [0,1,?] failed once, making 1 or the unknown teammate suspicious. Proposed team [0,1,3] keeps two likely good players (0,3) and adds only one uncertain player (1). As Good on quest 4, approving a team with a strong core is reasonable; rejecting risks cycling to worse teams and an auto-pass danger later. My vote reveals some trust in 0 and 3 and willingness to test 1, which is acceptable for Good.
Player 1: I’m Good and know only public info. Team [0,3] succeeded on quest 2, while [0,1] on quest 1 had exactly one fail, meaning at least one of 0 or 1 is evil. Since I know I am good, 0 is highly suspicious. This proposed team [0,1,3] includes 0, so it likely contains an evil player. In team voting, Good should reject teams with a strong evil candidate, especially on quest 4 where failure is dangerous. Rejecting also pressures a safer team composition, likely centering on 1 and 3 with someone other than 0.
Player 4: I know player 2 is evil with me. Quest 1 [0,3] failed with exactly one fail, so at least one of 0 or 3 is evil. Quest 2 [0,3] succeeded, which is consistent with an evil on that team choosing pass. Current proposed team [0,1,3] excludes both known evil me and 2; if both 0 and 3 were good this would likely succeed, which is bad for evil at score 2-1. But prior fail strongly suggests one of 0/3 is evil, making this team potentially good for us to approve because it may contain an evil saboteur while looking reasonable. As evil, I want the team to pass and, if an evil is on it, likely fail quest 4 to bring score to 2-2. Approving also avoids signaling too strongly that the team is good.
